Name: GATHER
LongName: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting (GATHER) statement
Description: GATHER defines minimum reporting requirements for studies that produce quantitative population-level health estimates by synthesizing multiple data sources with statistical or mathematical models.
Aim: To improve transparency, interpretability, and reproducibility of global health estimates by requiring explicit reporting of objectives, data inputs, analytical methods, uncertainty, results dissemination, and limitations.
ManuscriptSubType: Subnational or comparative population estimates study
Keywords: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting

Scope.Coverage: Studies that estimate health indicators across multiple populations, places, or time periods using multiple input datasets and formal synthesis/modeling methods.
Scope.Focus: Transparent reporting of indicator definitions, population/time coverage, data identification and selection, input-data characteristics and bias, model specification and selection, uncertainty quantification, access to input data and analytical code, and interpretation of estimate updates.
Scope.ContrastWith: Not intended for single-source descriptive reports (for example one survey round without multi-source synthesis), clinical trial reports, or general epidemiologic analyses that do not generate comparative population-level health estimates via modeling.

primary.description: Stevens GA, Alkema L, Black RE, et al. Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting: the GATHER statement. Lancet. 2016;388:e19-e23.

items.item_name: Define indicators, populations, and time periods
items.item_question: Are the indicator(s), populations (including age, sex, and geographic entities), and time period(s) for estimation clearly defined?
items.item_question_background: Explicit scope definitions are necessary to interpret comparability and intended policy use of estimates.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all target indicators, population strata (age, sex, geography), and covered time periods for the reported estimates.

source_page_refs.quote: Define the indicator(s), populations ... and time period(s) for which estimates were made.

items.item_question: Are funding sources for the work reported?
items.item_question_background: Funding transparency supports appraisal of potential influence and reporting completeness.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all funders and related support statements for the estimation work.

source_page_refs.quote: List the funding sources for the work.

items.item_question: For synthesized data inputs, is how data were identified and accessed described?
items.item_question_background: A documented search and access process is required to assess selection rigor and reproducibility.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract data search strategy, discovery channels, and access procedures for included synthesized datasets.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe how the data were identified and how the data were accessed.

items.item_question: Are inclusion and exclusion criteria specified, including ad-hoc exclusions?
items.item_question_background: Transparent eligibility criteria reduce risk of selective inclusion and hidden biases.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all predefined inclusion/exclusion rules and any ad-hoc exclusions applied during data processing.

source_page_refs.quote: Specify the inclusion and exclusion criteria. Identify all ad-hoc exclusions.

items.item_question: Are all included data sources and core characteristics reported?
items.item_question_background: Source-level metadata are necessary for judging representativeness and harmonization validity.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract, for each included source, reference/contact, represented population, collection method, years, sex/age range, diagnostic or measurement criteria, and sample size where relevant.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide information about all included data sources and their main characteristics.

items.item_question: Are categories of input data with potentially important bias identified and described?
items.item_question_background: Bias characterization supports appropriate uncertainty interpretation and cross-source synthesis decisions.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract any source categories flagged for bias risk and the rationale for each bias concern.

source_page_refs.quote: Identify and describe any categories of input data that have potentially important biases.

items.item_question: For additional non-synthesized data inputs, are data and sources described?
items.item_question_background: Covariates and supporting inputs can materially influence estimates and must be transparent.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract all additional analysis inputs (for example population denominators or covariates) and their data sources.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe and give sources for any other data inputs.

items.item_name: Shareable data-input file format and metadata
items.item_question: Are all input data provided in an efficiently extractable format, with metadata or access contacts for restricted data?
items.item_question_background: Machine-readable inputs and access pathways are essential for verification and reuse.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract where input datasets are shared, file format details, accompanying metadata, and contact/institution details when legal or ethical constraints prevent open sharing.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide all data inputs in a file format from which data can be efficiently extracted.

items.item_question: Is a conceptual overview of the analysis method provided (including a diagram when helpful)?
items.item_question_background: A conceptual map helps readers understand model flow before technical details.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract high-level narrative and any workflow diagram describing analytical pipeline and model structure.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide a conceptual overview of the data analysis method. A diagram may be helpful.

items.item_question: Are all analysis steps described in detail, including mathematical formulae and preprocessing/adjustment/modeling steps?
items.item_question_background: Technical reproducibility depends on complete step-level and mathematical reporting.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract detailed analytical workflow: cleaning, preprocessing, adjustments, weighting, and all mathematical/statistical model definitions.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide a detailed description of all steps of the analysis, including mathematical formulae.

items.item_question: Are candidate models and final-model selection procedures described?
items.item_question_background: Model-selection transparency is required to interpret estimate robustness and avoid opaque choices.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract criteria and process used to evaluate candidate models and justify final model choice.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe how candidate models were evaluated and how the final model(s) were selected.

items.item_question: Are model performance evaluation results and relevant sensitivity analyses reported when done?
items.item_question_background: Performance diagnostics and sensitivity checks determine trustworthiness and stability of estimates.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract performance metrics/validation outputs and any sensitivity-analysis results with interpretation.

source_page_refs.quote: Provide the results of an evaluation of model performance, if done, as well as the results of any relevant sensitivity analysis.

items.item_question: Are methods for calculating estimate uncertainty described, including uncertainty sources included and omitted?
items.item_question_background: Uncertainty reporting is central for policy interpretation of model-based estimates.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract uncertainty methodology, interval computation details, and explicit list of uncertainty sources accounted for and not accounted for.

source_page_refs.quote: Describe methods of calculating uncertainty of the estimates.

items.item_name: Access to analytical or statistical source code
items.item_question: Is access information provided for analytical or statistical source code used to generate estimates?
items.item_question_background: Code accessibility materially improves reproducibility and methodological auditability.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract source-code availability statement, repository or URL, licensing/access constraints, and any key-code segment availability notes.

source_page_refs.quote: State how analytical or statistical source code used to generate estimates can be accessed.

items.item_question: Are published estimates provided in an efficiently extractable file format?
items.item_question_background: Machine-readable outputs enable downstream validation, reuse, and synthesis.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract where final estimates are published and whether provided in machine-readable formats (for example CSV/XLS rather than only PDF).

source_page_refs.quote: Provide published estimates in a file format from which data can be efficiently extracted.

items.item_question: Is a quantitative measure of estimate uncertainty reported?
items.item_question_background: Point estimates without uncertainty can mislead decision-making and overstate precision.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract uncertainty intervals or equivalent quantitative uncertainty metrics for reported estimates.

source_page_refs.quote: Report a quantitative measure of the uncertainty of the estimates.

items.item_name: Interpretation against existing evidence and estimate updates
items.item_question: Are results interpreted in light of existing evidence, including reasons for changes from previous estimates when applicable?
items.item_question_background: Contextual interpretation is necessary to distinguish true epidemiologic change from method-driven revisions.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract interpretation comparing findings with existing evidence and, if prior estimates exist, explicit reasons for estimate changes.

source_page_refs.quote: Interpret results in light of existing evidence. If updating a previous set of estimates, describe the reasons for changes.

items.item_question: Are limitations discussed, including modeling assumptions or data limitations that affect interpretation?
items.item_question_background: A transparent limitations discussion is essential for responsible use in policy and research.
items.item_extraction_instruction: Extract key model assumptions, data limitations, and their expected effect on interpretation of estimates.

source_page_refs.quote: Discuss limitations of the estimates. Include a discussion of any modelling assumptions or data limitations.

Aliases: Guidelines for Accurate and Transparent Health Estimates Reporting
EvidenceExamplesPositive: We estimated national and subnational under-5 mortality from 1990-2024 by synthesizing multiple survey and vital-registration sources, reporting inclusion criteria, model selection, uncertainty intervals, and code access.

This analysis provides age-sex-country prevalence estimates using a Bayesian hierarchical model with explicit data-source metadata, ad-hoc exclusions, sensitivity analyses, and downloadable estimate files.
Updated global burden estimates are presented with reasons for changes from prior rounds, detailed input-data processing, and transparent uncertainty propagation across modeled components.
EvidenceExamplesNegative: A randomized trial compares two hypertension treatments and reports participant outcomes without multi-source population estimation methods.
A single hospital cross-sectional survey reports local prevalence at one timepoint with no multi-population modeling or uncertainty framework for global estimates.
A qualitative policy paper discusses national health planning and data gathering challenges but does not produce quantitative modeled health estimates.
Matching.Explain: Use this as the primary design guide for studies producing model-based population health estimates across locations or time from multiple data inputs, emphasizing transparent inputs, methods, uncertainty, and reproducibility artifacts.
